title: DIY Mudroom System with Towers and Bench
summary: Two frameless storage towers plus a matching bench, all built from plywood strips and dressed up with real base moulding — a full mudroom wall in one project.
description:
Three separate plywood boxes — two tall towers and a bench — designed to sit together as one built-in-looking wall unit, even though nothing about the construction requires them to be permanently joined. Each tower is frameless (no applied face frame), which means the back panel is doing real structural work here, not just closing up the back: without it, a frameless box like this can rack out of square the first time someone leans on a door or overloads a shelf.

Base moulding along the bottom and edge moulding on the bench are what elevate this from "plywood boxes" to something that reads as built-in furniture — skip them and it still functions, but skip them at your own risk if the goal is a finished look. The bench doors are optional; a plain center divider with no doors is a legitimate simpler finish if hanging doors and hardware isn't where you want to spend your time on this build.

tools.note: For checking the towers and bench are square and true, especially if securing to a wall.

materials.name: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Have it ripped into 15-3/4" wide, 8 ft long strips at the store.

cutList.note: Space at least one center partition — a bench this wide sags without it.
Optional — cut to fit the actual opening.

steps.title: Cut the plywood parts
steps.body: Have the three 3/4" sheets ripped into 15-3/4" x 8 ft strips at the store — each sheet yields three strips, so you have nine 96" strips to cut everything from. Crosscut four tower sides 72", fourteen tower shelves 16", four tower cubby dividers 7-5/8", two bench top and bottom pieces 36", six bench dividers 34-1/2", and two bench sides 17-1/4". Those parts add up to roughly 856" against 864" of strip, so there is essentially no waste allowance here: lay the cuts out on paper first, put each 72" tower side on its own strip, and fill the 24" tail behind it with one 16" shelf and one 7-5/8" cubby divider. A fourth sheet is cheap insurance against one bad cut. From the 1/4" sheet cut two tower backs 17-1/2" x 72" plus a bench back sized to the bench box once it is built. Cut the 3-1/4" base moulding to 17-1/2" for each tower and 36" for the bench, and the edge moulding to 36".
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

steps.title: Build the two towers
steps.body: Each tower is a 72" box: the two 72" sides run the full height and the 16" shelves fit between them, which makes each tower 17-1/2" wide overall — the same 17-1/2" as its back panel and its base moulding, so those two parts are your check that you have it the right way round. Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of every shelf, holes facing what will become the underside, and join with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue. Fix at least one shelf permanently near the middle of each tower: a 6 ft frameless box with nothing but adjustable shelves has no fixed rail holding its width, and one fixed shelf gives it one. Keep the sides parallel as you work — measure the gap at the top, middle and bottom and make all three read 16".
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

steps.title: Add the shoe cubbies
steps.body: Each tower gets two of the 7-5/8" cubby dividers, notched into each other or simply screwed together, to make the plus shape that splits one bay into four shoe cubbies. Build each plus as its own small assembly on the bench and then slide it into the tower rather than fastening it in place. Two reasons that order matters: you can size the assembly against the opening you actually ended up with rather than the one on paper, and it stays removable if you want to reconfigure the shelving later. If you would rather it stayed put, a couple of 1-1/4" pocket screws up through the shelf below will hold it without being visible.
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

steps.title: Attach the tower backs
steps.body: Before nailing anything, square each tower up: measure both diagonals across the open back and push on the long one until the two match, because the back will lock in whatever shape it is nailed to. Then set the 17-1/2" x 72" back on and fasten it with 1-1/4" brads every 6" into both sides and into every fixed shelf. Backing a frameless tower is not cosmetic here — with no face frame, that thin back is the only thing resisting racking once the tower is loaded and somebody leans on a door or hangs off a shelf.

steps.title: Build the bench box
steps.body: The 36" top and bottom set the bench's 36" front — the same 36" as the base moulding and the edge moulding, which is your check — and the two 17-1/4" sides give it its height. Join with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, or nails and glue, whichever you used on the towers, and check the diagonals before the last fasteners go in. Then add at least one full-depth vertical partition at the centre, cut to the inside height of the box you just built rather than to a number: an unsupported 36" span of 3/4" plywood will sag under sitting weight over time, and that partition is also what a pair of bench doors will close against.
materials: Plywood, MDF, or particle board, 3/4", 4x8 sheet

steps.title: Back the bench
steps.body: Square the bench box the same way — both diagonals equal — then cut a 1/4" back to the box's actual outside dimensions and nail it on with 1-1/4" brads every 6" into the top, bottom, sides and the centre partition. The plan gives no cut-list row for this piece, so take it off the finished box. It matters as much here as on the towers: this is the one part of the mudroom system that gets sat on, and a back nailed to a square box is what keeps it square under that load.

steps.title: Add base moulding
steps.body: Nail a 17-1/2" length of the 3-1/4" base moulding across the bottom of each tower and a 36" length across the bottom of the bench, using 1-1/4" brads into the box bottom and the sides. Those three fronts use 71" of the 7 ft length, so there is only about a foot left — if you want mitered returns down the outer sides, buy a second length before you start rather than discovering it after the first miter. Then glue and pin the 3/4" edge moulding along the front edge of the bench top. A solid moulding survives shoes and heels far better than iron-on edge banding, and the front edge of a mudroom bench is the single most abused surface in this whole system.

steps.title: Add doors or a center divider
steps.body: If you want doors, measure the two openings the centre partition left you and cut a door for each, sized to leave an even 1/8" gap all round — measure the real opening, because the plan's own numbers disagree here: the cut list shows one 18" x 36" piece of 1/4" plywood while this step calls for leftover 3/4" stock, and the two pairs of hinges and two knobs supplied mean two doors, not one panel. Dress each door's edges with a strip of decorative 1/4" x 2-1/2" stock, hang them on the hinges, and fit the knobs last so you can adjust the fit without them in the way. If doors are more than you want to take on, a plain centre divider glued and nailed in place is a perfectly good finish — and it is the same partition the bench top needs for support anyway, so you lose nothing structural by stopping there.

images.alt: Mudroom storage system with two towers and a matching bench
